g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about the Reduction Clause in OpenMP, a powerful feature for parallel computing.
g.text: Parallel Programming Made Safe and Efficient

g.data-narration: In parallel programming, when multiple threads access shared data simultaneously, race conditions occur causing incorrect results.
g.text: Thread 1: read sum=0, compute 0+5=5, write sum=5
Thread 2: read sum=0, compute 0+3=3, write sum=3

g.data-narration: The reduction clause solves this by creating private copies for each thread and combining results safely at the end.
g.text: Automatically handles parallel reduction operations by:
1. Creating private copy for each thread
→ Combining all results at the end

g.data-narration: The reduction clause uses this syntax: reduction operator variable, creating thread-private copies automatically.
Each operator has an identity value - the neutral element that initializes each thread's private copy correctly.
Internally, reduction creates private copies, runs parallel execution, then combines results in a critical section.
Example: Summing 10 million elements. Each thread computes partial sums, then combines to get the final result.
Finding maximum value uses reduction max, starting each thread with the smallest possible integer.
For multiplication, the identity is 1. This example computes 20 factorial using parallel product reduction.
OpenMP supports multiple reduction variables in a single clause, allowing parallel computation of sum and product together.
g.text: for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) { sum += a[i]; product *= b[i]; }

g.data-narration: Avoid these common mistakes: forgetting the reduction clause, wrong identity value, and loop-carried dependencies.
Remember: reduction is more efficient than atomic, identity values are crucial, and clause placement matters.
g.text: • Memorize identity values: 0 for +/-, 1 for *, INT_MIN/MAX for min/max
• Place reduction on parallel construct, not inside loop body
• Reduction is faster than atomic - sync only at the end
• min/max written as reduction(min:var), not reduction(:var)min

g.data-narration: Reduction is optimized for combine-at-end pattern, making it faster than atomic or critical sections for parallel loops.
In summary: reduction clause enables safe parallel operations with private copies and synchronized combination at the end.
g.text: ✓ Reduction = Private copies + Parallel execution + Synchronized combination
✓ Identity values are crucial: 0 for ±, 1 for *, INT_MIN/MAX for min/max
✓ Faster than atomic/critical - single sync point
✓ Essential for sum, product, max, min operations
